articleTitle: Effectiveness of exercise and nutrition interventions for cognitive function in older adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.
takeaways: Combined exercise and nutrition interventions produced a small but statistically significant improvement in global cognitive function (effect size of 0.15) in adults aged 65 and older without dementia
The cognitive benefits were observed across 14 studies involving 4,013 participants with an average age of 71.4 years, suggesting these interventions work for typical older adult populations

When studies with multiple intervention components were excluded, the effect remained significant but smaller (effect size of 0.12), indicating that focused exercise-plus-nutrition programs still provide measurable cognitive benefits

The interventions did not significantly improve specific cognitive domains like executive function or visual-perceptual ability, but rather provided broad improvements in overall cognitive performance
The modest effect sizes observed in healthy aging contexts suggest these combined interventions may help slow cognitive decline and maintain functional independence as a dementia prevention strategy
